chapters.title: The Beardless Trickster
chapters.text:
In a certain aul on the wide steppe there lived a beardless trickster named Aldar Köse. His eyes were sharp, his feet were quick, his hands were nimble — and his wit was nimbler still.
He owned no riches at all. Yet he loved nothing better than to tease and outwit the fools and the misers — and by his cunning alone he never once went without a fat ram for his supper.
The Young Shepherd: “Aldar! You are smiling again. Whose sheep will be yours by sundown this time?”
Aldar Köse: “Only the ones a greedy man is too greedy to keep, little brother. Watch and learn.”
Old Auntie: “Leave old Zhatyrbai a sheep or two, you rascal — though heaven knows he would not do the same for you.”

chapters.title: Two Rubles and a Fat Ram
chapters.text:
One day Aldar came to the great tent of the greedy Bai Zhatyrbai, holding two silver rubles tight in his fist.
Aldar Köse: “Honoured bai, I have two rubles but no purse. My hand is tired of holding them. Help me be rid of them — sell me some poor little goat-kid for two rubles.”
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Ha! Gladly, gladly! Two rubles for a kid — a fine bargain. Here, take this one.”
The bai caught a scrawny, limping little kid — one that had two days of life left in it at most — and handed it over for the two rubles. But Aldar kept the coins in his fist.
Aldar Köse: “You know what, honoured bai — I have changed my mind. Take the two rubles AND the kid, and give me a lamb instead.”
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Two rubles and a kid for a lamb? Ha, not a bad trade at all! Take the smallest one.”
Aldar Köse: “Wait — I have changed my mind again. Take the rubles, the kid AND the lamb, and give me a she-goat.”
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Rubles, kid AND lamb, all for one thin goat! Heh — take her!”
Aldar Köse: “One last change, honoured bai. Take the rubles, the kid, the lamb and the goat — and give me a single ram. But let me choose it myself.”
The bai could hardly believe his luck — two rubles, a kid, a lamb AND a goat, all for a single ram! So Aldar walked into the flock and chose the very biggest, fattest ram, hoisted it onto his shoulders, and strolled away whistling.
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Ha-ha! What a fool that Aldar is — all that, given away for one single ram!”
Aldar Köse: “Sleep well, honoured bai. One of us is a fool — and it will take you a while to work out which.”

chapters.title: The Magic Fur Coat
chapters.text:
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Aldar! How can you whistle? Your coat is more hole than cloth — you must be frozen through!”
Aldar Köse: “Ah, honoured bai, that is the whole secret. My coat is warm BECAUSE of the holes. The wind blows in through this one, and straight out through that one, and never has time to chill me.”
Aldar Köse: “But your coat is closed all round, so the cold creeps in and stays, with nowhere to escape. That is why you shiver.”
Bai Zhatyrbai: “Of course! What a fool I have been! Aldar — trade with me! My fox-fur for your magic coat, quickly!”
Aldar Köse: “This coat? Oh, I could never part with it... but for you, honoured bai — since you ask so kindly.”
They swapped coats on the spot. Aldar rode home snug in the fine fox-fur — while Bai Zhatyrbai, colder now than ever, sat in a coat full of holes and understood, at last, that he had been tricked again.

chapters.title: Aldar and the Shaitans
chapters.text:
In a dark hollow at the edge of the steppe lived shaitans, who robbed every traveler by daring them to contests of strength. Aldar walked in armed with nothing but his wits.
The Shaitan: “Squeeze water from this stone, little man — or we shall have your bones!”
Aldar Köse: “Water from a stone? Child's play. Watch closely.”
Aldar palmed a soft white curd cheese in place of the stone, squeezed — and whey ran dripping through his fingers. The shaitans gasped.
The Shaitan: “Then race my swiftest brother across the steppe!”
Aldar Köse: “I am far too grand to run myself. I will send my baby brother — he naps in the grass over there.”
The 'baby brother' was a hedgehog. Each time the shaitan raced past a bush, another spiny little brother was already there ahead of him. Beaten at every turn, the shaitans fled in terror — and left all their gold behind. But first, one set Aldar a final riddle.
The Shaitan: “One question, trickster: what beats us every time, though it carries no club and no knife? Answer, and the gold is yours.”

chapters.title: The Trick That Fed the Aul
chapters.text:
Aldar carried the ram, the fine coat and the shaitans' gold home across the steppe — and shared nearly all of it among the poorest families of the aul.
The Young Shepherd: “Teach me, Aldar — how is it you always win, with nothing in your hands?”
Aldar Köse: “It is the simplest thing, little brother. A greedy heart always cheats itself. I only hold the door open and let it walk through.”
Old Auntie: “Bless your beardless chin, you rascal. The aul eats well tonight because of it.”

chapters.title: The Poorest, Happiest Man on the Steppe
chapters.text: Aldar shared the ram, the coat and the shaitans' gold among the poor of the aul, and kept for himself only the laughter. That night the dombra played until dawn. And so the beardless trickster stayed the poorest and the happiest man on the whole steppe — while the misers, one after another, never did learn.
